turns.content: I'm trying to port my legacy C library to Rust, and I keep thinking benchmarking will just be like running a simple loop in Python or JavaScript. If I can use criterion::Criterion the same way I used the console.log approach before, then I should be able to measure throughput easily without complex setup.

That's a reasonable intuition since you're familiar with simple timing loops in other languages. However, criterion::Criterion isn't just about running a loop; it's designed around Rust's ownership model and avoids the overhead of manual allocation or global state that plagues those simple approaches. When you use criterion::Criterion, it manages warm-up phases automatically, which prevents your code from being skewed by cold starts—a common issue in naive timing loops.

But isn't the throughput calculation just total work divided by time? That's exactly what I'm doing now with my stopwatch. If criterion::Criterion does that, why do I need to think about benchmark groups or parametric benchmarks at all?

Throughput is indeed total work over time, but the real value of criterion comes from how it structures your tests. When you use benchmark groups with parametric benchmarks, you aren't just measuring one point; you're systematically varying parameters like thread count or data size to see how performance scales under different conditions. This reveals bottlenecks that a single timing loop would miss.

Wait, so I'm wrong about it being just a simple loop? But if the goal is just comparing before and after my port, why do I need to worry about these complex groupings when I could just run two separate timing tests?

You're right that you can compare before and after, but using criterion::Criterion ensures both runs share the same warm-up behavior and measurement strategy. This eliminates noise from environment differences or compiler optimizations that vary between runs. The why here is consistency: without this shared context, your 'before' and 'after' measurements might not be apples-to-apples because you're introducing variability through different execution paths.

Okay, so the key difference isn't just in how I run the code but in how criterion::Criterion enforces a consistent measurement context. That makes sense for avoiding false positives. Does this mean I should avoid using std::time::Duration for my own measurements?

Exactly. While you can use Duration, relying on it directly exposes you to the same problems that naive timing loops solve: inconsistent warm-ups and lack of statistical significance. criterion::Criterion handles these details internally so your Rust code focuses on measuring actual throughput rather than debugging measurement artifacts.
I'm back in the game but I can't figure out how to use criterion::Criterion properly for a parametric benchmark comparing two different algorithm implementations.

You need to set up a criterion::BenchmarkGroup with a name and then iterate through your test cases using the block method. You can run multiple iterations per group and collect the results into a vec before calling the finalize function to get the mean, stdev, and other statistics.
That sounds too verbose for what I need. Why do you have to write all that boilerplate when I just want to measure throughput? Can't I pass a closure directly?

You can use the block method with your closures but you still need to manage the BenchmarkGroup lifecycle and handle the timing manually if you aren't using the built-in time measurement. The criterion crate provides helpers like mean, stdev, and median on the collected results so you don't have to parse raw nanoseconds yourself.
I still think this is overkill. My old code just ran the function and I got the elapsed time. Why am I struggling with these groups and throughput metrics when it seems so much simpler?

Your intuition about simplicity is correct for a single run but you are missing the statistical confidence that criterion provides. When you use benchmark groups you can compare before and after implementations reliably because criterion normalizes the warmup iterations and accounts for cold starts, which manual timing often misses.

I'm trying to switch from Python's timeit to Rust but I keep getting stuck on why criterion::Criterion uses groups and throughput instead of just a simple run count. It feels like the documentation is hiding something important about how measurements actually work.

You are right to be frustrated because that exact confusion trips up many developers coming from scripting backgrounds who expect raw execution counts. The key difference is that criterion::BenchmarkGroup and the throughput metric handle noise reduction automatically by running your function multiple times in a single measurement cycle, whereas simple run counts often amplify jitter. You need to understand how benchmark groups aggregate results internally before you can trust any throughput number.

Okay so if I write my loop manually and add it to the criterion::Criterion setup, why does the tooling insist on using a specific syntax for adding blocks? Is this just boilerplate or is there something else happening under the hood?

It's not just boilerplate; you are dealing with how criterion::Criterion manages the actual execution context and timing precision. When you add a block to your benchmark group, it ensures that warm-up runs happen before real measurements start so your first few iterations don't skew the throughput calculation. If you skip this step, your raw loop counts will be inflated by compiler optimizations and memory initialization costs.

I still can't get my benchmark to show the same result as Python's timeit even though I have the same algorithm. Is criterion::Criterion actually broken or am I misunderstanding how to interpret the output?

You are not misunderstanding; you are likely comparing apples and oranges because raw loop counts don't account for the overhead of function calls that Python's timeit ignores. When you look at your throughput, remember that criterion::BenchmarkGroup normalizes against the number of iterations per group to give you a rate per second rather than just total operations. Your manual implementation might be missing those hidden costs that make your raw count appear higher or lower depending on how much CPU is spent initializing.
So I need to add more iterations in my criterion::Criterion setup to match Python's results? That sounds like a workaround instead of fixing the algorithm itself.

Exactly, and that is why you should be looking at throughput instead of raw counts. If you keep adjusting iteration counts without understanding how criterion::BenchmarkGroup aggregates data, you will never get a fair comparison against Python's timeit because the two tools measure different things fundamentally. You need to ensure your Rust code accounts for the same initialization costs and that your benchmark groups run enough times so statistical noise doesn't distort the throughput metric.

I'm trying to benchmark my new batch processor and I keep thinking it's just like the old manual timer approach we used before, but now I need proper throughput metrics. The problem is when I run multiple iterations of a parametric benchmark with different workloads, the results seem unstable because I don't know how to structure the criterion::Criterion correctly.

You're right that manual timers are noisy and unreliable for production decisions. The real issue is that you're likely running single-threaded iterations or not separating warmup from measurement phases, which makes your throughput numbers look inconsistent. When using criterion::Criterion with benchmark groups, you should configure the group to run multiple replications and measure throughputs rather than just wall-clock times per iteration. This way, criterion will automatically discard warmup periods and only report stable measurements for each workload variant.

Hey! I'm working on my personal side project this weekend and I keep getting confused about how criterion::Criterion actually works compared to the standard time crate. Can you explain what's the difference between using Criterion for macro-based benchmarking versus just calling std::time::Instant?

Great question! Think of it like this: std::time::Instant is a stopwatch that starts and stops at specific moments, but you have to manually record the start time, run your code, then stop the timer and calculate the difference yourself. Criterion, on the other hand, is more like a gym with a dedicated trainer—it handles the warm-up phase automatically so your measurements aren't skewed by cold-start overhead, and it runs multiple iterations to give you a stable average instead of just one noisy result.

That analogy makes sense about the warm-up. But I'm still struggling with how exactly do I configure the benchmark groups in Criterion to measure throughput versus latency? Do I need to manually call a trait like IntoBench or something else to tell it what metric I want?

You don't use IntoBench; that's an older pattern. With Criterion you define groups using the Builder, which lets you specify different measurement targets like throughput (measuring items per second) or latency (average time per item). The key is setting up your group with a name and then attaching the benchmark function to it via the run method so Criterion knows exactly what code to execute repeatedly.

Why does this even work this way? I literally just added a benchmark group to my criterion::Criterion and now the compiler is screaming about missing implementations. Is Rust really going to force me to write separate tests for every metric?

You are right that it requires explicit trait implementations, but you only need to implement the Benchmark trait once per group and criterion will handle the rest. The benchmark groups in your crate definition tell criterion which traits to look for, so if you properly implement those traits with the correct function signatures, everything connects automatically without needing separate test files.
I tried that but my throughput numbers are still wrong. I think I implemented the trait incorrectly or maybe I forgot something about how parametric benchmarks work in this crate?

The issue is likely with your function signatures for the benchmark trait implementation. You need to ensure each group's benchmark functions match the exact parameter types that criterion expects, otherwise it won't run parametric benchmarks correctly and you'll get those incorrect throughput measurements.
Wait, so I just need to check my function signatures? But how do I know which parameters are required for each group without looking at the documentation?

You can find the exact parameter requirements by checking your crate's benchmark groups definition. The criterion framework will validate these automatically when you run your benchmarks, so if you get those errors it means your function signatures don't match what the group expects.
